extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin (PT) is a thermal incubator system comprising an insulated styrofoam container (0.03 m³ volume), a heatbed (214×214×3 mm) for uniform heating, a fan (0.02 m³/s airflow) for air circulation, two internal temperature sensors, and one external temperature sensor. The system regulates temperature within the insulated container (target range: 20°C to ~26.85°C) by controlling the heatbed and fan. Key characteristics include thermal inertia modeling, energy requirements (0.04 kg air mass with 700 J/kg/K heat capacity), and open-source hardware/software availability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin (DT) provides monitoring (real-time temperature tracking via RabbitMQ), prediction (thermal inertia simulation with 4-parameter model), optimization (dynamic control of heatbed/fan via controller), visualization (data representation for analysis), and fault detection. Services target engineers/developers for system validation, enabling simulation-based analysis, state estimation, and adaptive control strategies. Capabilities include parameter calibration, performance validation, and integration with supervisory controllers.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Raspberry Pi 4 Model B serves as the controller with GPIO for sensor/actuator interfacing. RabbitMQ (message broker) enables real-time communication between controller, sensors, and DT. DS18S20 temperature sensors (±0.5°C accuracy) measure internal/external temperatures. MOSFETs and optocouplers decouple control signals from high-current loads. PCB design integrates hardware components. Simulation models (2-parameter and 4-parameter) predict thermal behavior. Open-source repository (https://github.com/INTO-CPS-Association/example-incubator) hosts hardware/software specifications.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time synchronization between physical and digital components via RabbitMQ message broker, with calibration processes executed during experiments. The DT uses historical or real-time data from the Physical Twin (PT) for what-if simulations, and Kalman filters operate in real-time to estimate heatbed states. Time scales for services include real-time visualization (dashboard updates) and periodic calibration (model A/B parameter adjustments).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Multiple DS18S20 temperature sensors (3 instances) on a single 1-Wire bus, with distinct models (Model A/B) for thermal inertia prediction. The DT framework may support multiple instances for parallel calibration experiments. Coordination mechanisms include RabbitMQ message passing between controller, low-level driver, and DT components.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Orchestrated architecture integrating RabbitMQ communication server, low-level drivers, controller, and Kalman filters for anomaly detection. The DT framework includes a self-adaptation loop that dynamically recalibrates Model B parameters and reconfigures controllers when new objects (e.g., cold water) are introduced. System-wide coordination involves data flow from PT to DT via RabbitMQ and co-simulation of historical/real-time data.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Integration with external systems via RabbitMQ (open-source message broker) for data exchange between PT and DT. Dependencies include the GitHub repository for hardware/software specs and calibration datasets. Interoperability standards are implied through RabbitMQ's use, but no explicit integration patterns with other DTs or enterprise systems are described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Digital Twin models include behavioral (differential equations for dynamic analysis), analytical (energy equations like Eq. 9 with parameters C_heater, C_air, G_heater, G_box), and geometric models. Data sources are sensors (temperature, voltage) and simulation experiments. Data management uses time series databases, calibration algorithms, and Kalman filters for state estimation. Models are calibrated via nonlinear least squares optimization (Scipy) and validated against experimental data trajectories. Model B (four parameters) provides higher fidelity for thermal inertia prediction.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators include a fan (controlled via MOSFETs with 4A current capability) and a heatbed (regulated by a controller with PID logic). Control mechanisms use Raspberry Pi GPIO with optocouplers for electrical isolation. Remote control interfaces are via RabbitMQ message broker. Actuation ranges: fan voltage 18V (Fig. 12), heatbed power via V*I*Δt. Safety constraints include thermal limits and fault detection algorithms. Addressing mechanisms use 1-Wire bus for sensors and GPIO pins for actuation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors: DS18S20 1-Wire digital thermometers (accuracy 0.5°C, range -55°C to 125°C, 64-bit unique IDs) with three deployed units. Measurement capabilities include temperature tracking. Spatial distribution covers incubator interior. Sampling frequency not specified but data transmission occurs via RabbitMQ at real-time rates. Data collection mechanisms include low-level drivers interfacing with sensors and logging to time series databases.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity requires parameter calibration (C_air=486.12 J/K, G_box=0.856 J/K for Model B). Validation methods include residual error minimization (least squares optimization) and comparison with experimental data trajectories. Uncertainty sources include sensor noise and model simplifications. Error correction uses Kalman filters for heatbed state estimation. Quality assurance processes involve automated calibration experiments and what-if simulations to validate predictive accuracy.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from physical sensors (DS18S20 temperature sensors) to digital twin via RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol). Data types include temperature readings (°C) with 0.5°C accuracy. Transmission frequency is continuous (real-time monitoring). Event triggers include temperature thresholds or calibration experiments. Data quality assurance involves calibration experiments (Document 6) and preprocessing steps like filtering. Data integration mechanisms use RabbitMQ as a message broker to aggregate sensor data into the communication server.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control flows from digital twin to physical components via RabbitMQ. Command types include fan speed control (12V/24V) and heatbed activation. Control message formats use AMQP protocols with JSON-like payloads. Validation mechanisms include parameter checks before command execution. Feedback systems use temperature sensor data to adjust control logic. Conflict resolution prioritizes safety-critical commands (e.g., emergency shutdown). Command execution confirmation is achieved through status messages from MOSFETs and hardware actuators.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Technical network infrastructure uses RabbitMQ (AMQP protocol) for communication. Network topology is client-server (Raspberry Pi as server, sensors/actuators as clients). Bandwidth requirements are low (real-time sensor data streaming). Latency is minimized via edge computing (Raspberry Pi 4). Reliability mechanisms include message acknowledgments and persistent queues. Security protocols are not explicitly mentioned but may involve TLS for RabbitMQ if configured.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Deployment infrastructure uses Raspberry Pi 4 (edge computing) as the host. Computational requirements include CPU for running the controller and communication server. Storage requirements involve time-series databases for logged sensor data. Scalability mechanisms include adding more sensors or nodes. Deployment models support edge-cloud hybrid (RabbitMQ can run on-premise or in cloud). Availability is ensured by Raspberry Pi's uptime and RabbitMQ's fault tolerance.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle includes development (model calibration with 2/4 free parameters), deployment (integration with RabbitMQ for real-time data streaming), operational phases (continuous monitoring via Kalman filter and anomaly detection), and adaptation (self-adaptation loop triggered by environmental changes, involving recalibration of Model B and controller reconfiguration). Lifecycle governance involves open-source tooling (GitHub repository) and version control through iterative model refinement.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering processes include model calibration experiments (e.g., optimizing C_air and G_box parameters), version control via open-source repositories, and evolution strategies like recalibrating Model B with new data. Testing validates model accuracy (e.g., Model B outperforms Model A in heatbed inertia prediction). Quality assurance uses simulation-physical twin comparisons and Kalman filter-based state estimation. DT evolution incorporates co-simulations for what-if scenarios and dynamic controller parameter optimization.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support includes real-time temperature visualization via dashboards, anomaly detection using Kalman filters correlating sensor data to heatbed states, and what-if simulations for controller optimization. Analytics process historical/real-time data streams (RabbitMQ) to generate insights on thermal inertia and system dynamics. User interaction involves dashboard visualization and automated controller reconfiguration based on calibration results. Confidence measures derive from model calibration accuracy (e.g., Model B's 0.87 G_heater parameter).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Complies with open-source standards (RabbitMQ, GitHub repository). Adheres to IoT protocols for 1-Wire temperature sensors and GPIO interfacing. Follows simulation standards for thermal modeling (e.g., differential equations in dynamic models). Interoperability is achieved through message brokers (RabbitMQ) and standardized hardware interfaces (DS18S20 sensors, Raspberry Pi GPIO).

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

### **6 Summary**


We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

specifications of the case study.

### **2 Incubator Case Study**


The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.


**2.1** **Hardware Setup**


We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order

running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.

_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

**3.3** **Calibration**


Although the dynamics models have been built, they contain free parameters. In order
to utilise the models, the models need to be calibrated for the incubator. The results of
the calibration are detailed in section 4.2, and here we detail the procedure.
A non-linear least squares solver was used to calibrate the parameters of the models
derived above. Then the models are evaluated using the estimated parameters. Afterwards the resulting behaviour is compared with a data trajectory that is obtained by
running an experiment of the incubator. Finally using an optimisation function inside
the python Scipy package [14] to obtain the values of the parameters. Such a function
finds the parameters that minimizes the residual error. When the residual error is small
enough, the optimised parameters can be obtained.
The principle of the package is based on least-square, it assumes that:


_y_ = _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) + _ϵ_ (10)


where _f_ ( _x, θ_ ) is the model needed to be calibrated, _θ_ are the parameters represented in
a vector in the model, and _y_ is the label or the measured data.
A cost function or an objective function can be provided as:


_J_ = �( _y_ _i_ _−_ _f_ ( _x_ _i_ _, θ_ )) [2] (11)


The objective is to acquire a minimal value of _J_, and at the same time, the corresponding _θ_ are the value needed. In order to minimise the cost function, the most commonly
used one is gradient descent. After multiple iterations, the value of the cost function
goes to a sufficient small value and the _θ_ are obtained simultaneously.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.
The publicly available repository [2] contains the up-to-date hardware and software
specifications of the case study.

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary**

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. Hardware,
software, and datasets are available online [6] . Using this example to set the terminology,
we proceeded to demystify the DT concept and its goals.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi.
